question_en: Who said 'I only know that I know nothing'?
context_en: Socrates (469-399 BC) is famous for this statement of intellectual humility. We know him through the writings of his disciple Plato.

question_en: Which school proposed 'the greatest good for the greatest number'?
context_en: Utilitarianism, founded by Jeremy Bentham and developed by J.S. Mill, maximizes total happiness. It is very influential in politics and economics.

question_en: Who wrote 'Thus Spoke Zarathustra'?
context_en: Nietzsche published 'Thus Spoke Zarathustra' (1883-1885), where he introduces the 'superman' (Übermensch) and the 'will to power'.

question_en: Which philosopher wrote 'The Republic'?
context_en: Plato wrote 'The Republic' (~380 BC), where he sets out his theory of the ideal state and the famous allegory of the cave.

question_en: Who wrote 'Das Kapital'?
context_en: Karl Marx published the first volume of 'Das Kapital' in 1867. Along with 'The Communist Manifesto' (1848), it founded Marxism.

question_en: What does 'cogito ergo sum' mean?
context_en: 'I think, therefore I exist' is Descartes's phrase (1637). Doubting everything, the only certainty is that there is an 'I' that thinks and doubts.

question_en: Who was Aristotle?
options_en: A disciple of Plato and teacher of Alexander the Great
context_en: Aristotle (384-322 BC) was a disciple of Plato and tutor of Alexander the Great. He founded the Lyceum and laid the foundations of logic, biology and ethics.

question_en: What is 'Occam's razor'?
options_en: An argument for the existence of God
The principle of parsimony: the simplest explanation is usually the correct one
context_en: Occam's razor (William of Ockham, 14th century) says entities should not be multiplied without need. In science: the simplest hypothesis is preferable.

question_en: Who formulated the 'categorical imperative'?
context_en: Immanuel Kant formulated the categorical imperative: 'Act only according to the maxim that you can will to be a universal law'. It is the basis of his deontological ethics.

question_en: What is existentialism?
options_en: The school that places individual existence before essence
context_en: Existentialism (Sartre, Camus, Kierkegaard) holds that 'existence precedes essence': we are what we decide to be, with total freedom and responsibility.

question_en: Who wrote 'Being and Nothingness'?
context_en: Jean-Paul Sartre published 'Being and Nothingness' in 1943, a founding work of atheist existentialism. 'Hell is other people' is another of his famous phrases.

question_en: Which philosopher said 'God is dead'?
context_en: Nietzsche proclaimed 'God is dead' in 'The Gay Science' (1882) to signal that traditional values no longer have a foundation in modern culture.

question_en: What is Stoicism?
options_en: The pursuit of pleasure as the supreme good
The philosophy that seeks virtue and indifference to suffering
context_en: Stoicism (Zeno of Citium, Epictetus, Marcus Aurelius) seeks virtue and tranquility by accepting what we cannot control and controlling our reactions.

question_en: What is empiricism?
options_en: The philosophy based on pure reason
The school that holds that knowledge comes from sensory experience
context_en: Empiricism (Locke, Hume, Berkeley) holds that all knowledge comes from experience. It opposes the rationalism of Descartes and Leibniz.

question_en: Who wrote 'Meditations' (the Stoic book)?
context_en: Marcus Aurelius, Roman emperor (AD 161-180), wrote his 'Meditations' as a personal diary of Stoic reflections. It was never written to be published.

question_en: What is nihilism?
options_en: The denial of all meaning, value or objective truth
context_en: Nihilism holds that life lacks meaning or objective values. Nietzsche described it as a consequence of the 'death of God'.

question_en: Who wrote 'The Social Contract'?
context_en: Jean-Jacques Rousseau published 'The Social Contract' in 1762. It influenced the French Revolution with ideas about popular sovereignty and the general will.

question_en: What did Plato hold about reality?
options_en: That there are perfect Ideas of which the world is an imperfect copy
That only what we can touch exists
context_en: Plato's theory of Ideas (or Forms) holds that the sensible world is an imperfect shadow of a higher world of eternal, perfect Ideas.

question_en: Who was Epicurus?
options_en: A Greek philosopher who sought happiness in moderate pleasure
context_en: Epicurus (341-270 BC) founded Epicureanism: the pursuit of pleasure (hedone) understood as the absence of pain and disturbance (ataraxia), not as excess.

question_en: What is Hegelian dialectics?
options_en: The thesis-antithesis-synthesis process by which history advances
context_en: Hegel proposed that history advances through conflicts (thesis vs. antithesis) resolved in transcending syntheses. Marx adapted this to historical materialism.

question_en: Which philosopher wrote the 'Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus'?
context_en: Wittgenstein published the Tractatus in 1921, holding that the limits of language are the limits of the world. He later revised his thought in the 'Philosophical Investigations'.

question_en: What is phenomenology?
options_en: The philosophy that studies conscious experience as it appears
context_en: Phenomenology (Husserl, Heidegger, Merleau-Ponty) studies the structure of conscious experience 'as it is given to us', without metaphysical presuppositions.

question_en: Who wrote 'The Prince'?
context_en: Machiavelli published 'The Prince' in 1513, a work of political philosophy that separates morality from politics. 'The end justifies the means' is associated with his thought.

question_en: What is pragmatism?
options_en: The philosophy that values ideas by their practical consequences
The defense of the free market
context_en: Pragmatism (Peirce, James, Dewey) evaluates ideas by their usefulness and practical consequences. It arose in the U.S. in the late 19th century.

question_en: Which philosopher wrote 'Summa Theologica'?
context_en: Thomas Aquinas (1225-1274) wrote the Summa Theologica, attempting to reconcile the Christian faith with the philosophy of Aristotle.

question_en: Who said 'Man is the measure of all things'?
context_en: Protagoras (~490-420 BC), a Greek sophist, held this relativism: truth depends on each person's point of view. Socrates opposed this idea.

question_en: Which philosopher wrote 'Metaphysical Meditations'?
context_en: Descartes published 'Metaphysical Meditations' in 1641. It introduces the method of Cartesian doubt to find indubitable truths, arriving at 'cogito ergo sum'.

question_en: What did the philosopher Heraclitus hold?
options_en: That fire is the origin of everything and everything is in constant change
That water is the principle of everything
context_en: Heraclitus of Ephesus (~500 BC) held that 'everything flows' (panta rhei) and that fire is the principle of the universe. His famous phrase: 'You cannot step twice into the same river'.

question_en: What is rationalism?
options_en: The philosophy based on sensory experience
The school that holds that reason is the main source of knowledge
context_en: Rationalism (Descartes, Spinoza, Leibniz) holds that reason, not the senses, is the source of true knowledge. It opposes the empiricism of Locke and Hume.

question_en: Who was Baruch Spinoza?
options_en: A Dutch rationalist philosopher who identified God with nature
A philosopher of the French Enlightenment
context_en: Spinoza (1632-1677) was a rationalist philosopher who identified God with Nature (pantheism). He was excommunicated from the Jewish community of Amsterdam.

question_en: What is Mill's utilitarianism?
options_en: Maximizing total happiness considering quality, not just quantity, of pleasure
Virtue as an end in itself
context_en: J.S. Mill refined Bentham's utilitarianism by distinguishing higher pleasures (intellectual) from lower ones. He also defended individual freedom in 'On Liberty' (1859).

question_en: What is analytic philosophy?
options_en: The philosophical school centered on the logical analysis of language
context_en: Analytic philosophy (Russell, Wittgenstein, Frege) focuses on the logical analysis of language and arguments. It dominates in the Anglo-Saxon world.

question_en: Who wrote 'The Critique of Pure Reason'?
context_en: Kant published 'The Critique of Pure Reason' in 1781. It proposes that knowledge arises from the combination of sensory experience and a priori categories of the understanding.

question_en: What is determinism?
options_en: The doctrine that every event is caused by prior events
context_en: Determinism holds that every event, including human actions, is caused by prior events. It opposes free will. Laplace imagined a 'demon' that would know the future.

question_en: Which philosopher developed the concept of the 'superman'?
context_en: Nietzsche developed the concept of the 'Übermensch' (superman) in 'Thus Spoke Zarathustra' (1883). The superman creates his own values beyond good and evil.

question_en: What is virtue ethics?
options_en: Ethics centered on the character and virtues of the agent, not on actions
context_en: Virtue ethics (Aristotle, MacIntyre) focuses on cultivating virtues like prudence, courage and justice to achieve eudaimonia (human flourishing).

question_en: Who was Simone de Beauvoir?
options_en: A French novelist unrelated to philosophy
An existentialist philosopher and pioneer of modern feminism
context_en: De Beauvoir (1908-1986) was an existentialist philosopher, writer and feminist. 'The Second Sex' (1949) is a founding text of feminism: 'One is not born, but becomes, a woman'.

question_en: What is the Socratic method?
options_en: The method of questions and answers (maieutics) to discover the truth
context_en: Socratic maieutics consists of asking questions so that the interlocutor discovers the truth for themselves. Socrates compared the method to the work of his mother, a midwife.

question_en: What is moral relativism?
options_en: The belief in universal moral values
The doctrine that moral values vary according to culture or individual
context_en: Moral relativism holds that there are no universal ethical truths: what is right depends on cultural or individual context. It opposes Kant's moral universalism.

question_en: Who was Michel Foucault?
options_en: A poststructuralist philosopher who studied the relationships between power and knowledge
context_en: Foucault (1926-1984) analyzed how power operates through knowledge, institutions and social practices. His works on prisons, madness and sexuality are essential.

question_en: What is Stoic philosophy regarding the emotions?
options_en: That negative emotions arise from false judgments and can be controlled with reason
That we must suppress all emotions
That emotions are the basis of morality
context_en: The Stoics believed that 'passions' (fear, desire, sadness) arise from mistaken judgments. Reason can correct these judgments and achieve ataraxia (tranquility).

question_en: Who wrote 'The Phenomenology of Spirit'?
context_en: Hegel published 'The Phenomenology of Spirit' in 1807, describing the path of consciousness from sensory certainty to Absolute Knowledge. It is one of the most difficult works in philosophy.

question_en: What is philosophical skepticism?
options_en: The stance that questions the possibility of certain knowledge
context_en: Skepticism (Pyrrho, Hume) doubts the possibility of knowing the truth with certainty. Descartes used methodical doubt to overcome it and reach the cogito.

question_en: What did Rousseau hold about human beings?
options_en: That man is evil by nature
That man is naturally good but society corrupts him
That man is a wolf to man
context_en: Rousseau held that human beings are good in their natural state but society corrupts them. His 'noble savage' contrasts with Hobbes, for whom life without a state would be 'nasty, brutish and short'.

question_en: What is Comte's positivism?
options_en: The school that holds that only verifiable scientific knowledge is valid
context_en: Auguste Comte founded positivism (19th century), which argues that the only valid knowledge is scientific and verifiable. He also coined the term 'sociology'.

question_en: Who was Confucius?
options_en: A Chinese philosopher and teacher who taught ethics, family and government
A god of the Chinese pantheon
context_en: Confucius (551-479 BC) was the most influential philosopher in China. His teachings on ethics, family and government are collected in the 'Analects'. Confucianism shaped East Asia.

question_en: What is Taoism?
options_en: A Chinese philosophy that seeks to live in harmony with the Tao (the natural way)
context_en: Taoism (Laozi, 6th century BC) proposes living in harmony with the Tao, the natural principle that governs the universe. Non-action (wu wei) is its key principle.

question_en: What is deontological ethics?
options_en: Ethics based on the consequences of acts
Ethics based on the fulfillment of duty, regardless of consequences
context_en: Deontological ethics (Kant) judges acts by their conformity with moral duty, not by their consequences. A good act is one done out of duty, not inclination.

question_en: Who was Averroes?
options_en: A medieval Arab philosopher who translated and commented on Aristotle
context_en: Averroes (Ibn Rushd, 1126-1198) was the great commentator on Aristotle in the Islamic world. His works greatly influenced medieval Christian scholasticism.

question_en: What is Buddhism?
options_en: A philosophy and religion based on the teachings of Buddha that seeks the cessation of suffering
context_en: Buddhism was founded by Siddhartha Gautama (Buddha, ~500 BC). The Four Noble Truths and the Eightfold Path guide toward nirvana, liberation from suffering.

question_en: Who was Alexander the Great's teacher?
context_en: Aristotle was the tutor of Alexander the Great between 343 and 340 BC, at the request of Philip II of Macedon.

question_en: Which Greek philosopher lived in a barrel?
context_en: Diogenes of Sinope, the Cynic, lived in a large jar (not exactly a barrel) in Athens. He despised social conventions.

question_en: Which philosophical school did Zeno of Citium found in the 4th-3rd century BC?
context_en: Zeno of Citium founded Stoicism in Athens around 300 BC. He taught in the Stoa Poikile (painted porch).

question_en: Which work did Boethius write in prison?
context_en: Boethius wrote 'The Consolation of Philosophy' around AD 524, before being executed on the orders of Theodoric.

question_en: Which French philosopher wrote 'Discourse on the Method'?
context_en: René Descartes published the 'Discourse on the Method' in 1637. A foundation of modern rationalism.

question_en: To which school does John Locke belong?
context_en: John Locke was one of the great representatives of British empiricism, along with Berkeley and Hume.

question_en: Which work did Locke write in 1689?
context_en: John Locke published 'An Essay Concerning Human Understanding' in 1689. He defended the tabula rasa at birth.

question_en: Who wrote 'Leviathan'?
context_en: Thomas Hobbes published 'Leviathan' in 1651, defending an absolute sovereign power to avoid the 'war of all against all'.

question_en: Which famous phrase sums up Hobbes's thought on human nature?
options_en: Man is a wolf to man
I only know that I know nothing
context_en: 'Homo homini lupus' (man is a wolf to man) condenses Hobbes's view of the natural state of violence.

question_en: Which work did David Hume write in 1739-40?
context_en: Hume published 'A Treatise of Human Nature' between 1739 and 1740. It represents the most radical skeptical empiricism.

question_en: What did Berkeley propose about reality?
context_en: George Berkeley defended subjective idealism: 'esse est percipi' (to be is to be perceived).

question_en: Who wrote 'Beyond Good and Evil'?
context_en: Friedrich Nietzsche published 'Beyond Good and Evil' in 1886. It criticizes the foundations of Western morality.

question_en: Which philosopher wrote 'The World as Will and Representation'?
context_en: Arthur Schopenhauer published this work in 1819. His pessimistic philosophy influenced Nietzsche and Wagner.

question_en: Which work did Hegel write in 1807?
context_en: Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel published 'The Phenomenology of Spirit' in 1807. It is a key work of German idealism.

question_en: Which work did Kierkegaard write in 1843?
context_en: Søren Kierkegaard published both 'Fear and Trembling' and 'Either/Or' in 1843. He is considered a precursor of existentialism.

question_en: Who is the father of Marxism?
context_en: Karl Marx (1818-1883) developed historical and dialectical materialism along with Friedrich Engels.

question_en: Which work did Marx and Engels co-write in 1848?
context_en: Marx and Engels published 'The Communist Manifesto' in 1848, commissioned by the Communist League.

question_en: Which philosopher wrote 'Being and Time'?
context_en: Martin Heidegger published 'Sein und Zeit' (Being and Time) in 1927. A fundamental work of contemporary philosophy.

question_en: Which philosopher influenced Heidegger from phenomenology?
context_en: Edmund Husserl was Heidegger's teacher and predecessor. He founded phenomenology, a key philosophical method of the 20th century.

question_en: Which French philosopher wrote 'The Myth of Sisyphus'?
context_en: Albert Camus published 'The Myth of Sisyphus' in 1942. An essay on the absurdity of existence.

question_en: Which French philosopher wrote 'The Second Sex'?
context_en: Simone de Beauvoir published 'The Second Sex' in 1949. A fundamental work of modern feminism: 'One is not born, but becomes, a woman'.

question_en: Who said 'Hell is other people'?
context_en: Jean-Paul Sartre wrote 'Hell is other people' in 'No Exit' (1944).

question_en: Which German-Jewish philosopher wrote about the 'banality of evil'?
context_en: Hannah Arendt coined the expression 'banality of evil' in 'Eichmann in Jerusalem' (1963), after witnessing the trial of the Nazi official.

question_en: Which German thinker wrote about 'Twilight of the Idols'?
context_en: Nietzsche published 'Twilight of the Idols' (Götzen-Dämmerung) in 1889.

question_en: What did Hume think about causality?
options_en: It is only a psychological habit
context_en: Hume held that causality is a mental habit: we observe constant succession of events, but not a necessary connection.

question_en: What did Karl Popper propose as a criterion of scientific status?
context_en: Karl Popper proposed falsifiability as a criterion of demarcation between science and pseudoscience.

question_en: Which philosopher of science wrote about 'paradigms' and scientific revolutions?
context_en: Thomas Kuhn published 'The Structure of Scientific Revolutions' in 1962, introducing the concept of 'paradigm'.

question_en: Which work did Machiavelli write in 1513?
context_en: Niccolò Machiavelli wrote 'The Prince' in 1513. A realistic treatise on political power.

question_en: Which Renaissance thinker defended 'the end justifies the means'?
context_en: This maxim is usually attributed to Machiavelli, although the literal formulation does not appear exactly in his works.

question_en: Which work did Thomas More write in 1516?
context_en: Thomas More published 'Utopia' in 1516. It describes an imaginary island with an ideal society.

question_en: Who said 'Sapere aude' (dare to know)?
context_en: Kant popularized 'Sapere aude' as the motto of the Enlightenment in his essay 'What Is Enlightenment?' (1784).

question_en: Which Greek philosopher was called the 'philosopher of fire'?
context_en: Heraclitus of Ephesus (6th century BC) proposed fire as the first principle. Famous for 'everything flows' (panta rhei).

question_en: Which pre-Socratic philosopher said 'Everything is full of gods'?
context_en: Thales of Miletus, the first philosopher according to tradition, held that everything was water and that the cosmos was animated.

question_en: Which philosopher said 'everything flows, nothing remains'?
context_en: Heraclitus defended permanent change: 'you cannot step twice into the same river'.

question_en: Which philosopher opposed Heraclitus by defending the unity of being?
context_en: Parmenides of Elea held that being is one, immobile and eternal. Change is illusory.

question_en: Which Greek philosophers defended atomism?
context_en: Leucippus and Democritus (5th century BC) proposed that reality was made of indivisible atoms moving in the void.

question_en: Which work by Spinoza was published posthumously in 1677?
context_en: Baruch Spinoza wrote the 'Ethics Demonstrated in Geometrical Order', published in 1677 after his death.

question_en: What is Spinoza's central idea about God?
options_en: God and nature are the same
context_en: Spinoza identified God with Nature ('Deus sive Natura'), defending pantheism.

question_en: Which German philosopher is famous for his 'Monadology'?
context_en: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz set out his theory of monads in 'The Monadology' (1714). Simple spiritual substances.

question_en: Who is the main philosopher of American pragmatism?
context_en: Charles S. Peirce, William James and John Dewey are the main representatives of American pragmatism.

question_en: Who is Ludwig Wittgenstein?
context_en: Wittgenstein (1889-1951) revolutionized the philosophy of language with the 'Tractatus' and the 'Philosophical Investigations'.

question_en: What is the last sentence of Wittgenstein's 'Tractatus'?
options_en: The world is all that is the case
Whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent
context_en: Proposition 7 of the 'Tractatus' (1921) says: 'Whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent'.

question_en: Which French philosopher wrote 'Discipline and Punish'?
context_en: Michel Foucault published 'Discipline and Punish' in 1975, on the history of punishment and modern techniques of power.

question_en: Which school did Jacques Derrida found?
context_en: Derrida (1930-2004) initiated 'deconstruction', a critique of the hidden hierarchies in philosophical texts.

question_en: Who is the father of structuralism?
context_en: Claude Lévi-Strauss applied structuralism to anthropology. Saussure had developed it earlier in linguistics.

question_en: Which work is the basis of linguistic structuralism?
context_en: The 'Course in General Linguistics' (1916) by Ferdinand de Saussure, published by his students, founded structuralism.

question_en: Which French philosopher wrote about 'the Symbolic, the Imaginary and the Real'?
context_en: Jacques Lacan, a French psychoanalyst, proposed the symbolic-imaginary-real triad to describe the human psyche.

question_en: Who is Habermas?
options_en: A German philosopher of the Frankfurt School
context_en: Jürgen Habermas (1929-) is one of the most influential living philosophers, associated with the Frankfurt School. Theory of communicative action.

question_en: Which philosophers formed the first generation of the Frankfurt School?
context_en: Theodor Adorno and Max Horkheimer are the main representatives of the first generation of Critical Theory.

question_en: Which work did Adorno and Horkheimer co-write?
context_en: 'Dialectic of Enlightenment' (1944) by Adorno and Horkheimer is one of the pinnacle texts of Critical Theory.

question_en: Which school did Sigmund Freud found?
context_en: Sigmund Freud (1856-1939) founded psychoanalysis in the late 19th century. 'The Interpretation of Dreams' (1900) is his central work.

question_en: Who is C.G. Jung?
options_en: A Swiss psychiatrist, a disciple of Freud
context_en: Carl Gustav Jung (1875-1961) founded analytical psychology. He introduced concepts like archetypes, the collective unconscious and the shadow.

question_en: Which Greek philosopher wrote the 'Nicomachean Ethics'?
context_en: Aristotle wrote the 'Nicomachean Ethics' dedicated to his son. It is the main ethical treatise of Greek thought.

question_en: Which Aristotelian virtue describes the 'golden mean'?
options_en: Every virtue is a golden mean
context_en: Aristotle defined each ethical virtue as a golden mean between two vicious extremes (e.g., courage between cowardice and recklessness).

question_en: Who is Thomas Aquinas?
context_en: Thomas Aquinas (1225-1274), a Dominican, fused Aristotelianism with Christian theology in his 'Summa Theologiae'.

question_en: How many proofs of the existence of God did Thomas Aquinas offer?
context_en: The Thomistic 'five ways' (motion, cause, contingency, degrees of perfection and order) attempt to demonstrate the existence of God.

question_en: Which philosopher is famous for the 'wager' about the existence of God?
context_en: Blaise Pascal formulated his famous 'wager': betting on the existence of God is the rationally most advantageous option.

question_en: Which work did Voltaire write in 1759?
context_en: Voltaire published 'Candide, or Optimism' in 1759, a satirical parody of Leibnizian optimism.

question_en: Which Enlightenment philosopher defended the separation of powers?
context_en: Montesquieu defended the separation of powers in 'The Spirit of the Laws' (1748). It inspired modern constitutions.

question_en: Which famous American thinker wrote 'Walden'?
context_en: Henry David Thoreau wrote 'Walden' (1854) after living for two years in a cabin by Walden Pond.

question_en: Which literary-philosophical movement did Emerson found in the U.S.?
context_en: Ralph Waldo Emerson led Transcendentalism in New England starting in the 1830s.

question_en: Who is Henri Bergson?
context_en: Bergson (1859-1941) proposed concepts like 'duration', 'élan vital' and 'intuition'. Nobel Prize in Literature 1927.

question_en: Which thinker proposed 'panta rhei'?
context_en: 'Panta rhei' (everything flows) synthesizes Heraclitus's philosophy of permanent change.

question_en: Who wrote 'The Rebel'?
context_en: Albert Camus published 'The Rebel' (L'homme révolté) in 1951.

question_en: Which 20th-century movement questions the 'grand narratives'?
context_en: Jean-François Lyotard defined postmodernity in 1979 as 'incredulity toward metanarratives'.

question_en: Which French philosopher wrote 'The Postmodern Condition' (1979)?
context_en: Jean-François Lyotard published 'The Postmodern Condition' in 1979, popularizing the term 'postmodernity'.

question_en: Which French philosopher developed the concept of 'simulacrum'?
context_en: Jean Baudrillard developed the theory of hyperreality and simulacra in works like 'Simulacra and Simulation' (1981).

question_en: Who is Mary Wollstonecraft?
context_en: Mary Wollstonecraft wrote 'A Vindication of the Rights of Woman' in 1792, a key work of early feminism.

question_en: Which thinker is famous for studying suicide sociologically?
context_en: Émile Durkheim published 'Suicide' in 1897, one of the first works of modern empirical sociology.

question_en: Which thinker wrote 'The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism'?
context_en: Max Weber published this essay in 1904-1905, analyzing the relationship between Calvinist Protestantism and modern capitalism.

question_en: Which French philosopher wrote 'Anti-Oedipus' with Guattari?
context_en: Gilles Deleuze co-wrote 'Anti-Oedipus' (1972) with Félix Guattari. A radical critique of psychoanalysis and capitalism.

question_en: Which American philosopher wrote 'A Theory of Justice' (1971)?
context_en: John Rawls published 'A Theory of Justice' in 1971. It introduces the 'veil of ignorance' and the principles of justice.

question_en: Who wrote 'Anarchy, State, and Utopia' (1974)?
context_en: Robert Nozick replied to Rawls in 'Anarchy, State, and Utopia' (1974), defending a minimal state.

question_en: Who is the father of contemporary Christian personalism?
context_en: Emmanuel Mounier founded the journal Esprit and developed Christian personalism in the 1930s.

question_en: Which Lithuanian-French philosopher wrote 'Totality and Infinity'?
context_en: Emmanuel Levinas (1906-1995) published 'Totality and Infinity' in 1961. He defended ethics as first philosophy, based on otherness.

question_en: Which philosopher wrote 'Being and Having' as a contrast to 'Being and Time'?
context_en: Gabriel Marcel published 'Being and Having' (1935), a work of French Christian existentialism.

question_en: Who is Paul Ricoeur?
context_en: Paul Ricoeur (1913-2005), a French philosopher, worked on hermeneutics, memory, narrative and ethics.

question_en: Which philosophical tradition does Gadamer represent?
context_en: Hans-Georg Gadamer (1900-2002) renewed philosophical hermeneutics with 'Truth and Method' (1960).

question_en: Which philosopher said 'we are condemned to be free'?
context_en: Jean-Paul Sartre wrote this in 'Existentialism Is a Humanism' (1946).

question_en: Which philosopher wrote 'Existentialism Is a Humanism'?
context_en: Sartre delivered this lecture in 1945 (published in 1946), defending existentialism as a humanist philosophy.

question_en: Which medieval Arab philosopher wrote 'The Incoherence of the Philosophers'?
context_en: Al-Ghazali wrote this critique against the philosophers in the 11th century. Averroes replied with 'The Incoherence of the Incoherence'.

question_en: Who is Avicenna?
context_en: Avicenna (Ibn Sina, 980-1037) was a Persian physician, philosopher and polymath. His 'Canon of Medicine' was taught in Europe until the 17th century.

question_en: Which medieval Spanish-Jewish philosopher wrote 'Guide for the Perplexed'?
context_en: Moses Maimonides (1138-1204), born in Córdoba, wrote the 'Guide for the Perplexed' in Arabic. It synthesizes the Jewish faith with Aristotelian philosophy.

question_en: Which work did Thomas More write in Latin in 1516?
context_en: Thomas More published 'Utopia' in 1516, a word he coined himself from Greek ('no-place' or 'good-place').

question_en: Who wrote 'The Praise of Folly'?
context_en: Erasmus of Rotterdam published 'Stultitiae Laus' (The Praise of Folly) in 1511, a moral satire of Christian humanism.

question_en: Which Greek philosopher ran the 'Academy'?
context_en: Plato founded the Academy in Athens (~387 BC). It operated until AD 529, when Justinian closed the pagan philosophical schools.

question_en: Which school did Aristotle found in Athens?
context_en: Aristotle founded the Lyceum in 335 BC. His followers were called 'peripatetics' because he taught while walking.

question_en: Which school did Epicurus found?
context_en: Epicurus founded the 'Garden' (Kepos) in Athens (~306 BC), a philosophical community that admitted women and slaves.

question_en: Who is Lucretius?
context_en: Lucretius wrote 'De rerum natura' (1st century BC), a poem in verse that sets out the atomist philosophy of Epicurus.

question_en: Who is Seneca?
context_en: Seneca (4 BC-AD 65), born in Córdoba, was a prominent Stoic philosopher, playwright and advisor to Nero.

question_en: Which Roman emperor was a Stoic philosopher?
context_en: Marcus Aurelius (AD 121-180) wrote the 'Meditations', a pinnacle work of Stoicism, in the midst of his reign.

question_en: Which Greek philosopher wrote the 'Handbook' (Enchiridion)?
context_en: Epictetus, a freed slave, wrote the 'Enchiridion' (Handbook), a compendium of Stoic ethics. It influenced Marcus Aurelius.

question_en: What is the Chinese Taoist philosophy?
context_en: The 'Tao Te Ching', attributed to Laozi (6th century BC), is the founding text of Taoism. It advocates spontaneity and wu wei.

question_en: Who is Confucius?
options_en: A Chinese philosopher of the 6th-5th century BC
context_en: Kong Fuzi (Confucius, 551-479 BC) founded one of the most influential philosophical-ethical schools in China. His work: the 'Analects'.

question_en: Which sacred Hindu text deals with duty (dharma)?
context_en: The Bhagavad Gita ('Song of the Blessed One') is part of the Mahabharata. A dialogue between Krishna and Arjuna about duty.

question_en: What is the philosophy of Buddha Siddhartha Gautama?
context_en: Buddhism is based on the Four Noble Truths about suffering and the Eightfold Path to overcome it.

question_en: Which Zen philosophical school is linked to Japanese Buddhism?
context_en: Zen, a Japanese Buddhist school (chan in Chinese), emphasizes meditation and sudden enlightenment (satori).

question_en: Which philosophical school does Nagarjuna represent?
context_en: Nagarjuna (~AD 150-250) founded the Madhyamaka school of Mahayana Buddhism, based on the doctrine of emptiness (shunyata).

question_en: Which medieval philosopher wrote 'The City of God'?
context_en: Saint Augustine of Hippo wrote 'De Civitate Dei' (AD 413-426) after the sack of Rome by Alaric.

question_en: Which work did the scholastic Anselm of Canterbury write about God?
context_en: Anselm of Canterbury wrote 'Monologion', 'Proslogion' (with the ontological argument) and 'Cur Deus Homo' (on the incarnation).

question_en: Who is the 'Subtle Doctor' among the scholastics?
context_en: John Duns Scotus (1266-1308), called the 'Subtle Doctor', was a Franciscan and an intellectual rival of Thomism.

question_en: Who formulated the 'razor' principle in the 14th century?
context_en: William of Ockham (1287-1347) formulated the principle of parsimony: 'do not multiply entities without need'.

question_en: Who is Giordano Bruno?
context_en: Giordano Bruno (1548-1600), an Italian Dominican, defended the infinity of the universe and was burned by the Inquisition.

question_en: Which philosophical school does Auguste Comte represent?
context_en: Auguste Comte (1798-1857) founded positivism and coined the term 'sociology'.

question_en: Which Marx-Engels work sets out dialectical materialism?
context_en: 'Dialectics of Nature' (1883), by Engels, explores applications of dialectical materialism to the sciences.
